problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem B (Australia wildfire drone swarm). The team had to (1) determine the optimal number and mix of two drone types — SSA surveillance drones and VHF/UHF Radio Repeater (RR) drones — dispatched from mobile Emergency Operations Centers (EOCs) to fight Victoria bushfires, balancing cost, speed, and coverage; (2) explain how the mix adapts to extreme fire events over the next decade; (3) optimize RR drone hovering positions across different terrains (plains, mountains, urban); and (4) write a 1-2 page annotated budget request to the Victorian government.
models_used: Model I — Rasterized Multi-Objective Optimization Model: study area discretized into 10km grids labeled as Barrier/Roaming/Wildfire grids; grid label N = INT(x/Gs) + M*INT(y/Gs) with center-coordinate recovery formulas

Wildfire Frequency-Distance Weighted scoring for EOC siting: SC_k = sum_i N_i * (1/d_ki), ranking 33 candidate roaming grids and selecting top 4 EOCs, with a custom de-clustering correction (penalize EOCs within <=3 grids of each other by subtracting u * average SC)

Multi-Objective Optimization with 0/1 decision variables x^(alpha)_ki, x^(beta)_ki: dual objectives min-max flight distance (proxy for completion time) and min total drone count, combined via weighted method min max[ mu*sum(d*x) + (1-mu)*sum(x) ]*xi, subject to endurance (t_fly + t_hover <= 2.5h) and range constraints (RR <=30km, others <=20km)

Model II — Poisson Process for extreme fire events: P(N(t)=n) = (lambda*t)^n e^(-lambda*t)/n!, lambda estimated from ~50 years of historical bushfire data; expectation xi = sum n*p(n) = 2.99431 ~ 3 events per decade, fed back as the scaling factor xi in Model I's objective
Cubic Spline Interpolation to smooth the drone-count and time-vs-fire-area response curves (areas 1x to 10x unit area)

Model III — Hovering Model based on Tabu Search (TS): neighborhood mapping D -> D_i (i=1..4), candidate set + tabu list + aspiration/amnesty, layer-by-layer neighborhood traversal to minimize path cost omega; applied separately to mountain and urban terrains
Radio signal attenuation model l = l_0 - mu*h (linear decay with altitude), attenuation coeff ~0.4 in mountains vs higher in cities (50% urban attenuation, 2km urban range)
Haversine formula for lat/long-to-distance conversion accounting for Earth curvature
Sensitivity analysis on xi (xi+0.05,...,+0.2) and robustness test injecting +-5% random noise into distances d_ki over 1000 simulations (95.7% drones still reach target, max time error 3.2657%)
key_techniques: Rasterization/discretization of a continuous geographic region into a grid as the unifying spatial abstraction reused across all three models (matches the 'discrete' problem type)
Frequency-weighted, inverse-distance facility-location heuristic for EOC placement, plus a hand-rolled de-clustering correction to enforce spatial balance the raw ranking lacked
Min-max objective to encode 'fastest completion' as minimizing the worst-case (longest) drone route rather than the average
Linear scalarization (weighted sum) of two objectives with a tunable mu calibrated by simulation, explicitly justified over staged optimization on time-cost grounds

Coupling two models through a single shared parameter: the Poisson expectation xi becomes the demand-scaling multiplier in the optimization objective, so the 'future extreme events' answer is mechanically wired into the drone-count answer
Custom pseudocode for the Poisson PDF because 'Python has no built-in' (Monte-Carlo-style 10000-iteration sampling) — shows their own algorithm rather than a black box

Terrain-specific modeling: barrier grids for impassable mountains (800m altitude threshold), altitude-dependent signal attenuation, and EOCs placed on north/south sides of ranges since drones can't overfly peaks
Heavy use of folium/Mapbox/OpenStreetMap visualizations (heat maps, choropleth altitude map, coverage circles, route arrows) layered on real geography
Two-pronged model validation: sensitivity (worst-case xi increase only) AND robustness (random distance perturbation with a quantified failure rate)
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on coherent architecture and deliverable discipline rather than raw mathematical depth. Three models are not bolted together — they share one rasterized grid and one coupling parameter (xi from Poisson feeds the optimization), so the paper reads as a single integrated framework that answers all four sub-questions in order. It directly and concretely hits every prompt requirement: a defensible number/ratio (4 SSA + 4 RR, ~1:1, doubled to 16 for charging cycles), a forward-looking adaptation answer grounded in 50 years of data (xi~3), terrain-specific hovering via Tabu Search, AND a polished, itemized budget letter addressed to the Victorian government with a cost-breakdown table — the exact final deliverable, which weaker teams often shortchange. It grounds everything in real, cited data (VIIRS fire alerts, NASA altitude, Kaggle coordinates, Global Forest Watch) and named real places (Bendigo Airport boundary, Melbourne, Mount Buffalo), giving it credibility. Both sensitivity and robustness are reported with hard numbers (max time error 3.2657%, 95.7% success), signaling a model the team actually stress-tested. The visualization density and clear section flow make it easy and pleasant for a tired judge to follow."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet names all three models up front with the headline numbers (2.99431 extreme events, robustness error 3.2657%) — judge gets the whole arc in one read. Literature Review is unusually strong for MCM: it surveys UAV path-planning by space dimensionality and planning method (Grid vs Road Sign vs Artificial Potential Fields) with a strengths/weaknesses tree, then explicitly chooses the grid method — motivating the core modeling decision instead of asserting it. Assumptions are each paired with an explicit Explanation justifying it. Section 4 (Model Preparation) cleanly separates data sourcing, Haversine distance conversion, and drone descriptions before any modeling. The Flow Chart (Figure 3) maps the entire paper. Section 6.2's interpretation of the count/time/ratio curves (why RR grows first, why SSA overtakes past 9x area) shows physical reasoning, not just plots. The Budget Request is a genuine annotated letter with a summary cost table — a clean, self-contained deliverable."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Severe scope truncation: the optimization is only actually solved for ONE southeast sub-area (147.29-149.15E), and mountain/urban results are shown for a couple of spots — 'due to limited pages' is invoked repeatedly to skip the actual computation, weakening the claim that 4+4 drones cover the region

Cost figures in the budget are essentially asserted, not derived: $160k/$970k/$830k/$570k appear with no per-drone unit cost model or transparent rollup, and the summary table has blank/inconsistent cells (Mountains and Metropolis 'Normal events' show 10,000 but totals are 830k/570k with no extreme column)

Constraint and notation sloppiness: several constraints (altitude, the alpha/beta range split in Eq. 10) are mentioned but not fully specified ('will be given directly in results'); LaTeX/OCR artifacts leave some symbols (psi, M, Gs definitions) garbled and the two sub-sections in Section 8 are both mislabeled 'Sensitivity Analysis'

The 'min max sum' objective (Eq. 6/8) is written ambiguously — it is unclear over which index the max is taken and how the per-EOC max combines, and the actual solver/optimization method for the 0/1 program is never named (just 'Python simulation')

Strong simplifying assumptions left un-stressed: weather/wind/humidity ignored, forward teams assumed co-located with the fire, EOCs freely placeable, mu calibrated 'by simulation' without showing the calibration — limits real-world fidelity

Data provenance glitch: the embedded choropleth table lists US states (Florida, Texas) rather than Victorian regions, suggesting a placeholder/figure-extraction error that a careful reviewer would catch
The min-max-distance = min-time proxy ignores hover time allocation, charging logistics beyond a crude 2x factor, and concurrent-mission contention
reusable_patterns: Rasterize-then-optimize: convert any continuous spatial problem into a labeled grid (passable/candidate/demand cells) so a discrete optimizer and clean visualizations both become available — a transferable spine for facility-location and coverage problems

Couple a forecasting model to a design model through one shared scalar: estimate future demand with a stochastic model (Poisson here) and inject its expectation as a scaling multiplier in the optimization objective, so 'future scenario' questions answer the 'design' question automatically
Inverse-distance frequency weighting (sum of N_i/d_ki) as a fast, explainable facility-siting score, then add a de-clustering penalty to fix the spatial imbalance greedy ranking creates

Encode 'fastest' as a min-max of the worst route, and combine competing objectives with a weighted sum whose weight is tuned by simulation — simple to implement under time pressure and easy to justify in writing

Validate with BOTH a directional sensitivity sweep (perturb the key parameter only in the worst-case direction) AND a Monte-Carlo robustness test (random noise over many runs) and report a concrete failure rate and max error — cheap to compute, very persuasive to judges
Literature-review-as-decision-tool: survey candidate methods, tabulate their strengths/weaknesses, then explicitly pick one — this both fills a required section and motivates your modeling choice
Treat the asked-for deliverable (here a budget letter to a government) as a first-class, polished, self-contained artifact with its own summary table, not an afterthought
Lead the summary sheet with model names plus the 2-3 headline numerical results so a judge grasps the contribution immediately
Use real, cited, named data sources and real geographic landmarks to buy credibility on a problem with no provided dataset
